pullRequest.title: Enable fingerprints updating through API
pullRequest.body:
- [x] I have manually reviewed the changes and fully understand the code.

Close #515 

We're also making the headers of fingerpritns from the API editalbe, it should be good to close #544.

We're upgrading libcurl-impersonate to support header reordering, so close #335, close #642.

pullRequest.title: Add support for riscv and armv7l wheels
Only include cookies in current request in response.cookies
bugfix: move curl_options to the end of setopt
Fallback to latin-1 for redirected url in Location header
enhance Curl class to handle None curl cases
pullRequest.body:
Probably fixes #599 

It’s kind of a workaround, but I couldn’t find a cleaner way to handle these abrupt cancellations/closures.

pullRequest.title: refactor: enhance Curl class cleanup methods
pullRequest.body:
It probably fixes #613 

- Added `clear_resolve` parameter to `perform` method for better resource management.
- Renamed `clean_after_perform` to `clean_handles_and_buffers` for clarity and updated its logic to handle resolve list cleanup.
- Added a call to `clean_handles_and_buffers` on `close` method.
- Updated calls to the cleanup method across the codebase to reflect the new method name and parameters.

pullRequest.title: Async WebSocket: Docs, Safety & Performance Improvements
pullRequest.body:
# Overview This PR follows on from #650, and focuses on improving features, safety, documentation and tests for the Async WebSocket code. Most of the changes are implementation details so they should be largely transparent to a user. This brings back a lot of the error handling from older versions, while also improving the overall architecture, features and performance of the current code. ## Summary - Fixed the vast majority of typing issues in `websockets.py` and surrounding files. - Removed Python 3.9 references and replaced with 3.10 where applicable - Improved WS benchmark README - Fixed bugs in WS benchmarks and refactor code - Added simple enum to change benchmark direction (recv/send/concurrent) - Added (optional) exponential retry with backoff on failed WebSocket message receive - Attempted to fix #614 in the sync Websocket implementation - Fixed spelling and typos - Completely overhaul scheduling system and replace with a simpler time based system - Adjusted WS queue sizes to reflect more realistic use cases - Improved and rewrote many docstrings - Changed the `not_set` singleton to a much more type safe one - Added RST docs for the websocket code - Added some more simple real world examples - Handled rare race where future object can be in an invalid state - Massively improved exception handling and error raising - both `send()` and `recv()` will now reliably raise errors - Added boolean parameter to fail connection if the receive queue fills up before messages are all consumed - Added context manager support to the Async Websocket - Added safety checks for ping to comply with spec - Added the option to drain the receive queue before raising exceptions when a connection fails - Added optional timeout parameter to all `send` methods - Improved `close()` and `terminate()` robustness - Improved locals caching in both read and write loops - Improved retry behaviour for recv - handle `ERRNO 11` from BoringSSL - Mapped empty server disconnect to `WebSocketClosed` - fixes #630 - Added defensive code if curl FD becomes invalid - Simplified read loop and switch to optimistic reading for better performance - Refactored write loop into two branch hoisted paths - default and coalescing path - Added the ability to send unlimited size frames using `CONT` flags - Handled zero byte sends from curl and add writer stall detection - Fixed race conditions when calling `flush()` - Added `max_message_size` parameter and size checking similar to AIOHTTP - Added detailed and comprehensive pytest suite generated by Claude - but manually reviewed ## Breaking Changes #### 1. `ws_connect` & `AsyncWebSocket` Parameter Changes The following arguments have been **removed** from `AsyncSession.ws_connect()` and the `AsyncWebSocket` constructor: * `retry_on_recv_error` (Replaced by the `ws_retry` object). * `yield_interval` (Replaced by `recv_time_slice` and `send_time_slice`). * `fair_scheduling` (Removed; fairness is now managed via time slices). * `yield_mask` (Removed). #### 2. Default Queue Size Reduction The default internal buffer sizes have been significantly reduced to lower memory footprint, improve backpressure and latency. This may impact applications relying on large internal buffers to handle bursty traffic without consuming it immediately. * **Recv Queue:** Reduced from `512` to `32` messages. * **Send Queue:** Reduced from `256` to `16` messages. * *Migration:* If your application needs a larger message buffer, simply increase the value passed to `recv_queue_size` / `send_queue_size` in `ws_connect`. #### 3. Ping Payload Validation The `ping()` method now strictly enforces RFC compliance for payload size. * **Old Behavior:** Allowed any size payloads (which would fail silently or at the curl level). * **New Behavior:** Raises `WebSocketError` immediately if the ping payload length is not between 0 and 125 bytes. #### 4. Import Path & Type Changes * **`WebSocketRetryStrategy`:** Users wishing to configure retry logic must now import this dataclass and pass it to `ws_connect(ws_retry=...)` instead of passing a boolean. #### 5. Behavioral Change in `recv()` Timeout * **Old Behavior:** `recv()` might hang indefinitely or behave inconsistently depending on the state of the background task. * **New Behavior:** `recv()` strictly enforces the `timeout`. If the timeout expires, `WebSocketTimeout` is raised. Additionally, if the transport fails, `recv()` will now raise the specific transport exception (e.g., `WebSocketError`) instead of a generic closure. ## Changes 1. **Cooperative Yielding:** Introduced `recv_time_slice` and `send_time_slice`. The read/write loops now track execution time and yield to the event loop after each time slice. This simplifies the scheduling and removes the bitmask operation checking. While writing this PR I also tested an operation check bitmask only approach, but the problem with that approach is that the uneven cost for `recv` vs `send` causes a collapse in concurrent throughput. Using a time slice is the simplest approach that still maintains decent concurrent performance. 2. **Retry Logic:** Introduced `WebSocketRetryStrategy`. This enables a user to configure their retry policy using a dataclass. They then pass that dataclass into the `ws_connect(ws_retry=...)` constructor and that policy is applied to WebSocket message receives. 3. **Drain on Error:** Added `drain_on_error=True`. If the transport fails, consumers can still read buffered messages from the internal queue before raising the exception. 4. **Error Handling:** Added `_finalize_connection` to ensure the transport enters a terminal state correctly. Exceptions are now cached and raised on subsequent calls to **both** `send()` and `recv()` instead of just `recv()`. This means you no longer have to concurrently call `recv()` to check for errors and `send()` will reliably raise errors as soon as it is called after an exception. - Why is this good? Let's say the server unexpectedly closes the connection while sending. Previously you wouldn't know since `send()` wouldn't be able to catch this transport exception. Now, the exception is raised immediately afterwards in the next send call, which effectively means it's detected almost instantly. 5. Added `__aenter__` and `__aexit__` support, making `AsyncWebSocket` a proper async context manager. 6. Introduced `_terminate_helper` to ensure clean shutdown of background tasks and proper cleanup. 7. **Max Message Size:** Added `max_message_size` enforcement (default 4MB) to prevent a server from sending a huge message which could crash a client and consume all the memory. This is the same as AIOHTTP. 8. **Refactored Read Loop:** While improving performance has not been a focus of this PR, users can expect a 5-15% performance improvement when continuously receiving messages. This is largely thanks to a simplified reader loop has been refactored to use optimistic reading. Instead of adding a reader on the FD then waiting for a notification that the FD is ready, it tries to read first, and on getting an EAGAIN, it then attaches the reader to the FD. This reduces the overhead for high-throughput streams when the next message is already available to read from the socket. Ubuntu:
Windows 11:
9. **Unlimited Message Size:** Huge messages are now split using the `CONT` flag, so they arrive as a single logical WebSocket message. This means you can send effectively unlimited size messages - if your server supports this. 10. **RST Docs & Unit Tests:** I've added RST documentation that should hopefully be clear, concise and easy to read for new users. The pytest unit tests were generated by Claude code, but I've gone through the suite by hand and cleaned up the tests. They cover a good range of test cases. All the tests are currently passing, even on Windows. I've also added some basic real world examples. ## TODO These are for a future contributor to investigate and implement solutions. - I noticed that [`curl_ws_send`](https://curl.se/libcurl/c/curl_ws_send.html) takes approximately 10x longer than [`curl_ws_recv`](https://curl.se/libcurl/c/curl_ws_recv.html). On my server, I can get around 920 MiB/s when receiving a continuous stream of messages (benchmark 1). When sending, I can't get any higher than 82 MiB/s. I've taken many cProfile performance captures and they all show that the `curl_ws_send` function is the bottleneck. It's not clear why it is this slow. Additionally, I believe that this has something to do with sending WS messages over TLS, as it seems to be a lot faster when sending with a plaintext WS. Someone should ideally performance profile the C code on the Curl side and understand what is happening that's causing it to take this long. What do we need to do to fix this? Send in a Curl patch? - When a `recv()` call is cancelled at the exact same time a message is available, it could lead to the message being dropped. This is because the message has already been retrieved from the receive queue, but it also can't return that message as the coroutine has been cancelled. This is a rare edge case, but it could lead to unexpected message loss. It might be tempting to think adding a deque for temporarily buffering that message and fetching it on the next `recv()` call is a good idea (LLMs will also suggest this), but doing this will lead to the sleeping waiter bug. Unlike most other WS libraries, we permit concurrent calls to `recv()`. Let's say there are two `recv()` callers. One of them is cancelled at the same time a message comes in. You add that message to a deque. That saves the message, but it doesn't wake up the other waiter, which is waiting on the receive queue. I suppose a solution here is use another async queue and wait on both queues at the same time but that's a messy approach which is likely best avoided. Either way, it's an issue but one which is rare enough that its not addressed in this PR. - Adding a keep-alive method Simple ping coroutine which pings the server once every `N` number of seconds. Libcurl normally consumes `PING`/`PONG` messages and responds automatically to a PING, so you won't see the PONG message (I think?). How do we know if the server is alive and working if we can't receive the PONG message in the first place? ## Closing Notes This PR was supposed to go up months ago, but due to gradual scope creep, testing complexities and ill health, it took much longer than I had expected. I hope this will be the last of the major changes and that it delivers meaningful value for the users of this library. The WebSocket code changes are written by hand but reviewed by a variety of LLMs. All testing is done manually on both Windows 11 and Ubuntu 24.04.3 LTS VMs. @ferdimsu @dolfies Apologies again for the ping, I know you guys have worked on this code before, so I was wondering if you'd have a moment to check out the changes and let me know if you had any feedback. Thanks!

pullRequest.title: Improve Async WebSocket
pullRequest.body:
# Overview This is a complete and fundamental rewrite of the Async WebSocket. This overhaul replaces the implementation with a fully asynchronous, task-based architecture that is idiomatic, highly performant, and hardened for production use. It now fails predictably and cleans up its resources correctly under all circumstances. This pull request also addresses several lifecycle and error-handling bugs in the AsyncWebSocket implementation. The key focus from this rewrite is to achieve the best possible performance while keeping the code clean and breaking changes to a minimum. With that said, there are some breaking changes, so let's get those out of the way. ## Breaking Changes 1. `send()` Error Handling is now asynchronous: - **Old**: A network error during a `send()` operation would raise an exception directly from the `await ws.send(...)` call. - **New**: A network error during a send is now caught by the background writer task. The exception is then placed on the receive queue and will be raised by a concurrent or subsequent call to `await ws.recv()`. Application-level error handling must be adapted to this new model. 2. `send()` is now non-blocking and Queued: - **Old**: `await ws.send(...)` would block until the data was passed to the OS socket buffer. - **New**: `await ws.send(...)` returns almost instantly after placing the message in an internal queue. To guarantee that all queued messages have been picked up for sending, callers must now use the new await `ws.flush()` method. 3. `recv_fragment()` Removed: The `recv_fragment` method is no longer part of the class. Message reassembly is now an internal implementation detail of the read loop. ## Performance The new implementation completely does away with the use of `run_in_executor`. The original issue I raised #645, found that the overhead from this was massive and taking up most of the time, leading to very low throughput figures. From reading the original code, it became clear that the Curl socket is not blocking and simply returns EAGAIN when it is not ready to be read from/written to. This implementation handles those EAGAINs and uses a similar behaviour to the `aselect(...)` to wait on FD availability. Instead of running the libcurl calls in a thread pool to avoid blocking, the code opts for a cooperative yielding approach, where every N number of I/O ops or after a certain time has elapsed, it will yield control to the event loop ensuring other things have a fair chance to run. This is obviously a double-edged sword. Providing too much time to the event loop will result in lower throughput, while taking up too much event loop time will starve the loop from being able to run the other applications. I've set values which I believe are a good balance of throughput and fairly yielding control. The current implementation uses background I/O tasks for reading and writing, which decouples the I/O operation from the public API. That means, when you call `send(...)` or `recv(...)`, you are simply performing a lightweight operation which adds the WS frame into an asyncio queue. When the FD is next available for reading or writing, it will pluck the frame from the queue and write it into the curl socket and vice versa. This tries to be the best of both worlds approach suitable for continnous and sparse messages by engaging CPU time only when it is needed and efficiently waiting at idle state. Due to the decoupling, it completely eliminates the crashing risk when calling send/recv concurrently. Since it is performing operations on an async queue, which by its very nature is designed for concurrent operations, there is no risk from crashing this way. As long as there is only one instance of the I/O loop tasks, there will only ever be one reader/writer interacting with the curl socket sequentially, removing this risk. On the write side, we are using some optimization techniques including adaptive batching and send coalesceing (optional) to try and reduce the overhead / number of system calls made. These techniques do actually work, but in this case, the benefits are limited, as there seems to be a hard cap on send throughput (around `40` MiB/s on my machine). During development, I've taken tens (if not hundreds) of different cProfile performance captures and no matter what I do, `send()` throughput is hard limited by curl's `ws_send()` call. It is likely that it cannot be improved further. In case you are wondering: Does the send coalescing actually work? Yes! Here is a cProfile with the flag turned on, sending `10` GiBs of data with a 65k chunk size: ``` 4385099 function calls (4379080 primitive calls) in 255.745 seconds Ordered by: cumulative time List reduced from 2431 to 40 due to restriction <40> ncalls tottime percall cumtime percall filename:lineno(function) 238/1 0.005 0.000 255.745 255.745 {built-in method builtins.exec} 1 0.000 0.000 255.745 255.745 ./client.py:1() 1 0.000 0.000 254.919 254.919 /home/.cache/pypoetry/virtualenvs/ws-bench-_cej2YY4-py3.13/lib/python3.13/site-packages/uvloop/__init__.py:51(run) 1 0.000 0.000 254.919 254.919 /home/.pyenv/versions/3.13.7/lib/python3.13/asyncio/runners.py:160(run) 1 0.000 0.000 254.918 254.918 /home/.pyenv/versions/3.13.7/lib/python3.13/asyncio/runners.py:61(__exit__) 1 0.000 0.000 254.918 254.918 /home/.pyenv/versions/3.13.7/lib/python3.13/asyncio/runners.py:64(close) 2/1 0.000 0.000 254.917 254.917 /home/.pyenv/versions/3.13.7/lib/python3.13/threading.py:1000(_bootstrap) 2/1 0.000 0.000 254.917 254.917 /home/.pyenv/versions/3.13.7/lib/python3.13/threading.py:1027(_bootstrap_inner) 2/1 0.000 0.000 254.917 254.917 /home/.pyenv/versions/3.13.7/lib/python3.13/threading.py:983(run) 1 0.214 0.214 254.905 254.905 /home/.pyenv/versions/3.13.7/lib/python3.13/asyncio/runners.py:86(run) 10189 0.722 0.000 252.518 0.025 /home/.cache/pypoetry/virtualenvs/ws-bench-_cej2YY4-py3.13/lib/python3.13/site-packages/curl_cffi/requests/websockets.py:1019(_write_loop) 10825 0.156 0.000 247.827 0.023 /home/.cache/pypoetry/virtualenvs/ws-bench-_cej2YY4-py3.13/lib/python3.13/site-packages/curl_cffi/requests/websockets.py:1086(_send_payload) 10188 0.092 0.000 247.613 0.024 /home/.cache/pypoetry/virtualenvs/ws-bench-_cej2YY4-py3.13/lib/python3.13/site-packages/curl_cffi/curl.py:522(ws_send) 10187 247.406 0.024 247.406 0.024 {built-in method curl_cffi._wrapper.curl_ws_send} 637 3.077 0.005 3.077 0.005 {method 'join' of 'bytes' objects} 628 0.001 0.000 1.953 0.003 /home/.cache/pypoetry/virtualenvs/ws-bench-_cej2YY4-py3.13/lib/python3.13/site-packages/uvloop/__init__.py:54(wrapper) 629 0.001 0.000 1.951 0.003 ./client.py:78(main) 629 0.293 0.000 1.951 0.003 ./client.py:30(run_benchmark_curl_cffi) ``` Here is another cProfile with the same transfer but without the feature: ``` 6694317 function calls (6688299 primitive calls) in 262.812 seconds Ordered by: cumulative time List reduced from 2418 to 40 due to restriction <40> ncalls tottime percall cumtime percall filename:lineno(function) 238/1 0.005 0.000 262.812 262.812 {built-in method builtins.exec} 1 0.000 0.000 262.812 262.812 ./client.py:1() 1 0.000 0.000 261.990 261.990 /home/.cache/pypoetry/virtualenvs/ws-bench-_cej2YY4-py3.13/lib/python3.13/site-packages/uvloop/__init__.py:51(run) 1 0.000 0.000 261.990 261.990 /home/.pyenv/versions/3.13.7/lib/python3.13/asyncio/runners.py:160(run) 1 0.000 0.000 261.989 261.989 /home/.pyenv/versions/3.13.7/lib/python3.13/asyncio/runners.py:61(__exit__) 1 0.000 0.000 261.988 261.988 /home/.pyenv/versions/3.13.7/lib/python3.13/asyncio/runners.py:64(close) 2/1 0.000 0.000 261.988 261.988 /home/.pyenv/versions/3.13.7/lib/python3.13/threading.py:1000(_bootstrap) 2/1 0.000 0.000 261.988 261.988 /home/.pyenv/versions/3.13.7/lib/python3.13/threading.py:1027(_bootstrap_inner) 2/1 0.000 0.000 261.988 261.988 /home/.pyenv/versions/3.13.7/lib/python3.13/threading.py:983(run) 1 1.920 1.920 261.976 261.976 /home/.pyenv/versions/3.13.7/lib/python3.13/asyncio/runners.py:86(run) 162871 1.041 0.000 257.854 0.002 /home/.cache/pypoetry/virtualenvs/ws-bench-_cej2YY4-py3.13/lib/python3.13/site-packages/curl_cffi/requests/websockets.py:1019(_write_loop) 325740 1.637 0.000 256.072 0.001 /home/.cache/pypoetry/virtualenvs/ws-bench-_cej2YY4-py3.13/lib/python3.13/site-packages/curl_cffi/requests/websockets.py:1086(_send_payload) 162870 0.935 0.000 253.749 0.002 /home/.cache/pypoetry/virtualenvs/ws-bench-_cej2YY4-py3.13/lib/python3.13/site-packages/curl_cffi/curl.py:522(ws_send) 162869 251.539 0.002 251.539 0.002 {built-in method curl_cffi._wrapper.curl_ws_send} 628 0.001 0.000 1.924 0.003 /home/.cache/pypoetry/virtualenvs/ws-bench-_cej2YY4-py3.13/lib/python3.13/site-packages/uvloop/__init__.py:54(wrapper) 629 0.001 0.000 1.923 0.003 ./client.py:78(main) 629 0.293 0.000 1.923 0.003 ./client.py:30(run_benchmark_curl_cffi) ``` Now the throughput is hard capped on my machine so the throughput is not that faster, but see the difference? That's `10188` calls to `ws_send()` with the feature vs `162870` function calls without. Also, more than `95%` of the time is spent in the blocking C function and there is little overhead from the Python > Note: These numbers were taken before the max frame size was set to `64k`, you can still get this benefit if you are aggregating frames smaller than this value. With streaming protocols which do not care about frame boundaries, this works nicely for smaller chunks. Here is the speed of that same transfer with `2048` byte chunk size without and with `coalesce_frames=True`:
On the Curl FFI side, I've made one key optimization which significantly speeds up receive throughput. Rather than creating a new `ffi.buffer(...)` object on reach fragment received, it now pre-allocates and reuses that existing buffer. This speeds things up a lot by avoiding repeated memory allocations in a tight loop. This is safe to do, since the buffer is read up to a bounded value, so it does not need to be cleared or allocated each time. Also, a bytes copy is made when the function returns so there are no referencing issues. I tried to make it return a `memoryview(ffi.buffer(...))` and it was quite a bit faster, but it ran into referencing issues since the view is pointing to the pre-allocated buffer. This means any changes there will change what all the memoryview instances are pointing to. Although the idea of bytes copying doesn't sound great, in reality, other than returning a no-copy view into the buffer, **all other methods are slower**. I tried returning `bytearray` objects or using a pre-allocated `bytearray` and extending the fragments into it, but that ends up being slower. In the end, having a list of bytes and calling `b"".join(chunks)` was the fastest way to collect up the fragments into one complete message. ### Benchmark Code Now let's get to the interesting part. The numbers tell their own story. In order to arrive at these numbers, I am running two different sets of benchmarks. 1. The first benchmark (`client.py` and `server_ssl.py`) simply sends and/or receives in a loop (depending on what code is commented out) and doesn't do anything other than time how long it took. 2. The second benchmark (`benchmark.py`) is slightly more realistic. It reads a file from disk to memory and loads its hash. Depending on if it was ran in server mode or client mode, it will receive or send that file and the other end will calculate a hash of the received data and compare against loaded hash of the file on disk and let you know if it matches or not. This will be slower due to the higher overhead. Neither benchmark is good and the benchmark code is bad. They are simply a rough proof of concept. I am using `btop` to monitor throughput. I am running each test more than once, noting that the first run is measurably slower sometimes, possibly due to the CPU cache. Either way a 5~10% variation on these figures should be factored in. I am using a HPE DL-60 Gen 9 rack server. This is an ancient server which clocks up to 1.6 GHz, so your results may vary. One slightly nice aspect is that I have 80 GB RAM on this server, hence I can run the benchmarks with 20 GB files loaded in memory without any issues. > In each test, I will compare the existing code vs new code from this PR. ### Files 1. [client.py](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/22858520/client.py) 2. [server_ssl.py](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/22858521/server_ssl.py) 3. [benchmark.py](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/22858617/benchmark.py) 4. [pyproject.toml](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/22858620/pyproject.txt) ### Slow Case The slow case is where the chunk size in all the benchmarks have been set to a small value, i.e. `2048` bytes. This increases the number of libcurl calls needed to transfer the same amount of data and slows down the throughput. #### Download `client.py`:
`benchmark.py`:
#### Upload `client.py`:
`benchmark.py`:
### Average Case The average case only considers `benchmark.py` results with a larger chunk size of `65536` bytes. #### Download
#### Upload
### Best Case These numbers are **unrealistic** and unlikely to be achievable in a real application. This only considers the `client.py` benchmark, which effectively just loops and does absolutely nothing with the received/sent data. Any application processing logic will create overhead and slow down the throughput. These figures are included here as an optimistic "what-if there was nothing else, how fast could we go?" situation. The chunk size is `65536` bytes. #### Download
My friend also ran this benchmark on his Windows machine which has a modern CPU that is about 10 years newer than my poor server: ``` --- curl-cffi Benchmark Complete --- Sent 200.00 GB of data in 91.74 seconds. Average throughput: 17.44 Gbps. ``` #### Upload
#### Concurrent Here is a test from the same benchmark, sending and receiving concurrently (without/with frame coalescing):
> The code provides lots of tunable parameters to tweak concurrent performance, such as `yield_interval`, `fair_scheduling` and `yield_mask`. All this can be set by an end user to suit their needs. ## Closing Note Should you trust my benchmarks and statements? Absolutely not. It's **_always_** best to run your own benchmarks (and double check the code yourself), since I have no idea what your use case is. I am benchmarking on `localhost` to eliminate network conditions as a factor, but it may be that real internet conditions impact the numbers significantly, in which case the relevant changes should be made. Also if you think the current `send(...)` api does not provide adequate guarantees of transmission, I can change this so that: - When `send(...)` is called, it inserts the content to be sent along with an `asyncio.Future` object into the send queue - The `send(...)` method then either returns a reference to the `Future` object or awaits it directly. - The write loop picks up this queue item and prepares it for transmission - After `ws_send(...)` is called, the `Future` result is either set to the number of bytes sent or an `Exception` object That would guarantee that the item has been sent out, but it would increase memory pressure and slow down the sending. So I think that should be considered. If that's needed it can be done as part of this PR or another follow up PR. I also need to think through how that'd work for coalesced sends. There is definitely room for improvement with this code and the architecture overall. I acknowledge that this is just one person's attempt at making a useful library better (although various LLMs were used to review the code and find improvements). Feel free to suggest improvements. I will do my best to integrate them and ensure the code is in the best state possible.

pullRequest.title: Add raise_for_status parameter to Session and AsyncSession
pullRequest.body:
- Add raise_for_status parameter (default: False) to both Session and AsyncSession
- Automatically raises HTTPError for 4xx/5xx status codes when enabled
- Similar to aiohttp's raise_for_status parameter
- Add 4 unit tests (2 sync, 2 async) to verify functionality
- Maintains 100% backward compatibility with default False value

Close #646

pullRequest.title: Upgrade websockets and FastAPI version in tests and dev
Fix websocket close code validation
pullRequest.body: This fixes the websocket close code validation to be less strict. Closes #672.

pullRequest.title: Improve wheels and sdist
pullRequest.body:
Replaces #192 

Excludes non-required and build-only files from bdist wheels.
Also, adds `tests` folder to sdist.

pullRequest.title: bugfix: Align exceptions in stream mode with non-stream mode
